explanation: The contract performs a low level .call or .delegatecall on an address taken from a function parameter. Letting callers choose the target (and often the calldata) turns the contract into a generic proxy: it can be made to call any contract with the caller's chosen payload, using this contract's identity, allowances, and balance. With delegatecall it is even worse, since the target's code runs in this contract's storage context and can overwrite anything, including the owner slot.
impact: An attacker can drain token approvals granted to the contract, steal its ether by directing calls to arbitrary contracts, or (via delegatecall) take full ownership of the contract by rewriting its storage.
